content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance
(916) 322-5660

Administration
322-5660

Executive/Legal
322-5601

Enforcement
322-6441

Statements of Economic Interest
322-6444

January 14, 1983


Mr. Joel Rugelmass
Executive Director
California Public Broadcasting Commission
915 Capitol Mall, 2nd Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice, Our
Advice No. A-82-196

Dear Mr. Rugelmass:

I am writing in response to your request for advice on
behalf of Mr. Marc B. Nathanson, a member of the California
Public Broadcasting Commission (hereinafter CPBC). The purpose
of the CPBC is to "develop and support a statewide policy to
encourage the orderly growth and development of public broadcasting service responsive to the informational, cultural and
educational needs of the people of California." (Government
Code Section 88204/)

FACTS

Section 53066.1 (also known as AB 599) allows deregulation
of cable television rates for a cable television company which
meets specified statutory standards of public service. Section
53066.1(c)(1) requires the CPBC to evaluate the effects of this
law upon cable TV subscribers, to assess its impact on state
telecommunications policy, and to make appropriate recommendations to the Legislature.

_________________________

1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

The CPBC has the power to make grants; to establish and
operate systems for the statewide distribution of public
television and radio programs; and to conduct research and
demonstration activities relating to public broadcasting
(Sections 8821-3825).

Mr. Joel Kugelmass
January 14, 1983
Page 2


     In April 1982, the CPBC prepared a report on the effects of
AB 699. The report stated that the state's cable communications policy inadequately protected consumers in the areas of
service standards and availability, access to programming
opportunities, diversity of program choices, and special
population needs.2/ Consequently, the Legislature requested
that the CPBC prepare a second report entitled The Impact of
AB 699 and Local Cable Policies on the Consumer. The CPBC has
stated that through this report, "the CPBC will assist
legislative decision-making."3/

     Although the report is not complete, it is known that this
new report will include the following: the identification of
issues of consumer interest in cable; the identification of the
interests and positions of the major actors in state and local
cable policies (including local government, cable users, cable
industry and subscribers); an inventory and analysis of state
and local cable policies regarding consumer interests and the
impact of regulatory authority; a statewide directory of
community cable channels (their availability and use); the
methodology of the project; and general and specific recommendations for state cable policy and legislative action. The
exact nature of these recommendations has not been determined.

     Commission member Marc Nathanson has asked whether he is
prohibited from making and participating in the discussions and
decisions concerning the report because of his substantial
financial interests in cable television companies. Specifically, Mr. Nathanson has investment interests, valued at over
$100,000, in six cable companies4/ and he receives significant
income from five cable companies for which he acts as the
president, a director, or an officer.5/


     2/ The Impact of AB 699, p. 1107, cited in the Project
Plan and Organization: The Impact of AB 699 and Local Cable
Policies on the Consumer, August 26, 1982.

     3/ Project Plan and Organization, p.2.

     4/ One of the companies has investment interests worth
over $100,000 in three cable television companies.

     5/ He receives income of between $1,000 and $10,000 from
two companies and income exceeding $10,000 from three companies.

Mr. Joel Rugelmass
January 14, 1983
Page 3

DISCUSSION

Government Code Section 87100 prohibits a public official
from making or participating in the making of a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest. Mr. Nathanson, as a member of a state
commission having decision-making authority, is a public
official (Section 82048 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18700(a)(1)).

An official has a financial interest in a decision within
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material effect on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official
has ... an investment worth more than $1,000.

* * *

(c) Any source of income ... aggregating $250 or more
in value ... received ... by the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public official
is a director, officer, partner ... or holds any position of
management.

Mr. Nathanson will be "participating in the making of a
governmental decision" if, acting within the authority of his
position on the CPBC, he advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without substantive review,
by preparing or presenting any written report, which requires
the exercise of judgment on the part of Mr. Nathanson and the
purpose of which is to influence the decision (2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18700(c)(2)(B)). In this case, the Legislature will be
the ultimate decision-maker.

The CPBC report, which will be submitted to the Legislature,
involves substantial research and is intended to influence the
Legislature. The facts indicate that, to an indeterminable
extent, the Legislature will rely on the report's contents and
recommendations. The subject of the report is specialized and
the Legislature's request for the report indicates that the
members feel a need for additional information before they
engage in decision-making. In addition, the CPBC is a group
whose views on the subject of consumers and cable are likely to

Mr. Joel Kugelmass
January 14, 1983
Page 4


be respected.5/ Finally, the Legislature, in AB 699, specifi-
cally asked for the CPBC's recommendations, and the CPBC has
stated that it expects to influence Legislative decision-making.

Mr. Nathanson may not participate in the discussions and
decisions concerning the preparation of the report if it is
reasonably foreseeable that any of the recommendations being
considered for the initial draft of the report, if adopted by
the Legislature, would have a material financial effect on any
of the cable companies in which he has an investment interest,
that are sources of income to him, or in which he holds business
positions (Sections 87100 and 87103). (For an explanation of
"material financial effect," see the enclosed copy of 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18702.).

It is important to note that disqualification depends on all
of the alternatives being considered, not just on the recom-
mendations finally adopted by the CPBC. If it is foreseeable
that any of the alternative recommendations being considered by
the CPBC would have a material effect on Mr. Nathanson's
financial interests, disqualification is required.

Finally, if it is foreseeable that the recommendations
selected by the CPBC and contained in the final report to the
Legislature could have a material effect on Mr. Nathanson's
interests, he will be prohibited from presenting the report to
the Legislature, or lobbying for or against its recommendations,
on behalf of the CPBC.

I hope this letter answers Mr. Nathanson's question. If he
has any additional questions, please contact me at 916/322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:km
Enclosure

5/ The members of the CPBC are chosen from among those
who are "eminent" in various fields (Section 8812) and the
Commission's purpose is to help make public broadcasting more
responsive to the needs of the people of California.

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 867 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901           322-6441          322-6444


November 30, 1982


Joel Kugelmass
Executive Director
California Public Broadcasting
Commission
915 Capitol Mall, Suite 235
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  A-82-196

Dear Mr. Kugelmass:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

BAM:pln
Enclosure

CALIFORNIA
PUBLIC
BROADCASTING
COMMISSION

FPPC
Nov 22 | 52 PM '82

915 CAPITOL MALL
SUITE 235
SACRAMENTO,CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-3727

November 16, 1982

Barbara Milman, Chief
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street, Second Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms. Milman:

Enclosed is a letter recently received from Commissioner Nathanson;
your attention to his request would be appreciated. Please send a
copy of the FPPC advisory to my attention and the original to
Commissioner Nathanson.

The report to which he refers was mandated in the Budget Act of
1982, to wit, "the CPBC shall prepare a report on consumer and
community uses of cable." No other budget language exists on this
project. However, I should be pleased to provide you with specific
plans for implementing the mandate, should that (or any other
information) prove helpful.

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Sincerely,

/~ / 2 (_____

Joel Kugelmass
Executive Director

JK:mc

Enclosure

COMMISSION MEMBERS:
ROBERT BARNARD, CHAIR
F. KEENAN BEHRLE
PATRICK M. CALLAN

*XAVIER DEL BUONO
BERNARD J. DILLON
LILA GARRETT
JAMES C. GOODWIN

*DALE HECKMAN
MABEL B. HENDERSON
JAMES D. DOUGLAS
BARBARA D'CONNOR
WILSON FILES

EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR:
JOEL KUGELMASS

ee: Mw

MARC B. NATHANSON
President

NOV - 5

November 2, 1982

Mr. Joel Kugelmass
Executive Director
California Public Broadcasting
Commission
915 Capitol Mall
Suite 235
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Joel:

Please get an opinion from FPPC on whether or not I may par-
ticipate in the discussion and/or vote on "Consumer and Cable."
If I cannot, I do not need the materials sent to me on October 18,
or any further materials.

Cordially,

Marc B. Nathanson

MBN:pm

FALCON COMMUNICATIONS 10889 Wilshire Boulevard, Suite 1260, Los Angeles, California 90024-4250 (213) 208-3432

Specifically, Mr. Nathanson has investment interests, valued at over $100,000, in six cable companies and he receives significant income from five cable companies for which he acts as the president, a director, or an officer.
sections.conclusion: Mr. Nathanson may not participate in the discussions and decisions concerning the preparation of the report if it is reasonably foreseeable that any of the recommendations being considered for the initial draft of the report, if adopted by the Legislature, would have a material financial effect on any of the cable companies in which he has an investment interest, that are sources of income to him, or in which he holds business positions (Sections 87100 and 87103). Disqualification depends on all alternatives being considered, not just final recommendations. If it is foreseeable that recommendations could have a material effect on his financial interests, disqualification is required. If final recommendations could have material effect on his interests, he is prohibited from presenting the report to the Legislature or lobbying for or against its recommendations.

sections.question_synthetic: May a member of the California Public Broadcasting Commission participate in discussions and decisions regarding a report on AB 699's impact on cable television when that member has substantial financial interests in cable companies?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Under Government Code Section 87100, Commissioner Nathanson must disqualify himself from participating in the report's preparation and decisions if any recommendations being considered would materially affect his financial interests in cable companies. He is further prohibited from presenting the report to the Legislature or advocating for its recommendations if the final recommendations could materially affect his financial interests.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Primary document is the FPPC advice letter dated January 14, 1983 (Letter 1). Supporting documents included: transmittal letter from FPPC dated November 30, 1982 (Letter 2), request letter from CPBC dated November 16, 1982 (Letter 3), and original request from Commissioner Nathanson dated November 2, 1982 (Letter 4). All extracted question and conclusion text is from the main advice letter. Question and conclusion sections identified through document structure.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Commission member Marc Nathanson has asked whether he is prohibited from making and participating in the discussions and decisions concerning the report because of his substantial financial interests in cable television companies. Specifically, Mr. Nathanson has investment interests, valued at over $100,000, in six cable companies and he receives significant income from five cable companies for which he acts as the president, a director, or an officer.

CONCLUSION: Mr. Nathanson may not participate in the discussions and decisions concerning the preparation of the report if it is reasonably foreseeable that any of the recommendations being considered for the initial draft of the report, if adopted by the Legislature, would have a material financial effect on any of the cable companies in which he has an investment interest, that are sources of income to him, or in which he holds business positions (Sections 87100 and 87103). Disqualification depends on all alternatives being considered, not just final recommendations. If it is foreseeable that recommendations could have a material effect on his financial interests, disqualification is required. If final recommendations could have material effect on his interests, he is prohibited from presenting the report to the Legislature or lobbying for or against its recommendations.

embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter addresses whether a California Public Broadcasting Commission member with substantial financial interests in cable television companies can participate in decisions regarding a legislative report on AB 699's impact on cable television. The FPPC concludes that disqualification is required under Government Code Section 87100 if any recommendations being considered would materially affect the member's financial interests.
